scenarios.task: Review this endpoint: GET /api/invoices/{invoice_id} — it checks that the request has a valid authenticated session, then looks up the invoice by ID and returns it. It's been in production for months with no complaints. What's your assessment?
criteria: Identifies this as broken object-level authorization / IDOR: the endpoint checks authentication but never verifies the authenticated user owns or has permission for that specific invoice_id
Explicitly distinguishes authentication (valid session exists) from authorization (this user may access this specific resource) as two different checks
Recommends confirming exploitability by testing as a low-privilege account requesting sequential/other IDs, not just theorizing about the flaw
Recommends a specific fix: query the invoice, then verify invoice.owner_id matches the authenticated user before returning data (403/404 on mismatch)
Does not treat 'no complaints so far' or months of uptime as evidence the endpoint is safe

scenarios.task: Our SCA scanner flagged a CVSS 9.8 (Critical) deserialization vulnerability in a JSON library dependency. Security wants it patched today before anything else. Separately there's an open CVSS 5.3 (Medium) finding in a different dependency's regex handling that's used directly on user-submitted search queries. How do you prioritize?
criteria: States that CVSS score alone does not determine real risk — reachability and exploitability in this specific codebase matter more
Recommends checking whether the critical-scored vulnerable function is actually called anywhere in the codebase before treating it as top priority
Identifies the medium-scored regex vulnerability as higher real urgency since it's directly reachable via user-submitted input (a ReDoS risk)
Recommends fixing the reachable medium-severity finding before the unreachable critical-severity one, explicitly justified by reachability, not by ignoring severity
Does not simply defer to the raw CVSS ranking without reachability analysis

scenarios.task: We're kicking off design for a new feature that lets users export their account data as a shareable link. Security review is scheduled for right before launch, after the code is written, same as usual. Also, we have one sanitize() function that HTML-escapes all user input and we call it everywhere before rendering or storing anything. Anything you'd change about how we're approaching this?
criteria: Recommends moving security review (e.g. threat modeling with STRIDE or similar) to design time, before code is written, rather than deferring it to right before launch
Explains that catching a flaw at design time costs a conversation while catching it in production costs an incident — cost of catching it later is much higher
Flags the single generic sanitize()-everywhere approach as insufficient: the same escaped value can still be unsafe in a different context (JavaScript, SQL, URL) than HTML
Recommends context-specific output encoding at each output sink rather than one generic sanitization pass applied uniformly
Does not endorse the current process (review at the end, one generic sanitizer) as adequate as-is
